hypothesis: If tensor completion-based surrogate models (CPD-S and NeAT), aggregated via a random-forest ensemble, are used to predict Young's modulus and mass-normalized Young's modulus from lattice design parameters, then they will produce higher mean R² and lower variance across runs than baseline ML methods (Gaussian Process, XGBoost, MLP) under both uniform random and biased (exponentially sampled) training regimes, particularly at small training sizes.
